sentences.text: Right.
And you know, he started to talk about his family.
And I was almost sure that he was Jewish.
He never said a word to me.
He asked me if I wanted to go with them, because-- to the-- and the army will take-- so I'll be better off.
And I said, no, I'm very happy here.
I want to stay here.
And-- so he really-- And so I said to myself, this guy, the way he talked to me and the way he showed me the picture, the boy who looked a little bit Jewish-- but I never asked him any questions.
He never asked me one single word about my family.
So that was an encounter.
But anyway, so there were no Jews in this area.
And I doubt very much if the servants ever dealt with any Jews, except those who were coming to the estate.
But once, I went to the field with them, and when the-- you know, during harvest time.
And there was a group of Jews led by Nazis.
At that time, they were bringing them to different points where they accumulated more Jews.
And then they put them on trucks and took them to a camp.
So they would walk them.
And I don't know from what area they were coming.
And one of them, right away, spotted that I was Jewish.
I mean-- you know, to you, it might be different, but-- hard to understand-- but people were so sensitive about appearances that they maybe got more experience.
And they they-- they learned from practice.
They knew already how to look at Jews and-- and how to recognize Jews.
And out of all people, he approached me.
And you know, I knew right away that he is Jewish.
And I'll tell you, I was scared to death, because the worst thing to do is to be with other Jews.
Once you-- at that time, we all knew that if you're standing with two, three other Jews, you'd all be spotted.
So you just try to stay away from Jews as much as you can.
So he came over and started asking me these silly questions.
What kind of village it is, what are the directions?
Anyway, I gave him the-- and I ran away.
So this is the only time that I saw very, very sad faces on those Jews.
They knew where they are going.
And that was really very sad.
There were about 30, and young people.
And of course, around there, there were a lot of communities with a-- a lot of bad things were happening at that time wherever there were a few Jews.
How did you'll hear about them?
Later on.
Yeah.
In the immediate area, no, because they were not reading any papers.
And I want you to know that the peasants never mentioned the word 'Jew' at all.
So again, I didn't know, is it because they don't want to let me know that they suspect, or they didn't know?
Whatever it is, it was never even mentioned.
And the only thing is they started to talk once when I was there.
And saying, you know, some Jews are crazy because they want to join the partisans in the forest.
And they-- they act so silly, because they know that the partisans will kill them before the enemy.
So I said to myself, are they giving me a message-- I should never try to run away?
Because a lot of people ran away to the partisans, and were killed, I found out later.
And it was dangerous for Jews to be there.
So there were some partisans, they accepted Jews.
But some didn't.
So that's where the-- but even right after the war, they never mentioned to me that they knew I was Jewish.
And they really treated me very, very well.
How did you live as a Catholic?
Yeah, I went to church with them.
I prayed with them, and I-- because I had my prayer book.
And that prayer book, by the way, I told you before, saved my life.
One day, one of the girls comes over to me and says, you know, so-and-so, a Polish policeman, is coming to-- to see you.
I said, why is he coming to see me?
Well, he suspects that you are Jewish.
This was at the Austrian--
Yeah.
So I started to laugh.
Suspect that I'm Jewish?
Let him come.
So she says, well, he'll be here this afternoon.
And I knew him.
I knew him because he used to come very often to the farm.
He was a friend of those people.
You know, there's some managers on the farm, and the accountants, so-- and he knew [INAUDIBLE].
Not Tony, the servant, other people who worked, and-- so-- And he came.
So I asked that Genia, if I can go into her room.
And she says, yes.
And I had all my papers prepared.
But I had my papers in a little, like, bag with this.
I had my prayer book on top of the papers.
He says, what is this?
I says, what?
I made believe I didn't know what he said.
So he said, well, this.
I said, you're not talking about the prayer book?
He says, yes.
I-- I said, what's-- why is this the question?
He says, I didn't know you have a prayer book.
I said, I promised my mother before I left that I will always have my prayer book, and I always go to church.
And then he asked me for my papers, and looks at them.
And he-- he says, OK.
And he walked out, and said to the girls, I don't care who she is.
She's a nice kid.
Let her live.
And later on, I found out that he was an expert in finding Jewish girls, especially women.
And before he killed them, he would-- if he wanted to cut off the breast, he would just cut off the nipple.
He would then make a few funny incisions.
If he wanted to do the-- the finger, he would first cut off the nail.
And I mean, he really tortured them before he killed them.
And he was very, very brutal.
He told them that I'm a nice girl, and he wants me to-- so it's all fate.
I mean, it's unbelievable.
So this was it.
This was my only encounter there with-- with trying to find out if I was Jewish or not.
What was it like to live from day to day with this kind of--
You mean there?
You know, I-- I was really clever.
First of all, I got to know their mentality.
And I said to myself, I have to show them that I know more than they, and they will somehow get respect.
So I started to read a book, which-- it taught how to make tricks, and how to predict the future from cards.
And I was so good.
Don't ask me now-- I wouldn't know.
I was so good I started to predict.
And believe it or not, each prediction came true.
And they were running to me from the village.
And they really wanted me.
And I was sitting with them.
And each prediction-- of course, I knew some of them already.
And I knew their dreams and hopes.
So I made up stories based on their hopes and dreams.
And I knew some of them had boyfriends, and some didn't.
As a matter of fact, one boy-- a peasant family, his boy fell in love with me.
And every night he came and held hands with me.
And the mother was only hoping that after the war I'll marry him.
And so they really-- they-- they made me very comfortable.
However-- They made you--
Comfortable.
No, how would I feel-- as a matter of fact, one of them, the servants there-- either it was jealousy-- because, after all, she was there for a long time, and I came not long ago.
And I was already entering the major's house.
Nobody else could, except that Genia and myself.
That was a big honor.
So she-- once, she asked me to help with the potatoes, to boil potatoes.
I never knew.
My mother never let me into the kitchen.
She said, you don't have to know how to cook.
So I made a mistake.
I rinsed them in cold water.
And there was a big dinner.
As a matter of fact, a lot of guests.
And I rinsed them with cold water.
And she saw.
She went crazy.
She-- she hit me.
And I thought she'll kill me.
Her whole face-- when she-- when she had a temper, forget it.
And if they didn't tear us apart-- the only thing is I stood up to her.
And that was good.
I didn't get scared, show her any fear.
And--
Yeah, that was the main thing.
And if somebody says-- you just laugh it off and go on.
And don't tell me, could I do it now?
I don't even know.
You know, in this kind of situation, you do things that you don't believe you could do it.
I must tell you, when I talk to you, I say to myself I make up stories, because I don't know how you did it.
I mean, I don't know how.
My sister went to work.
And I never mentioned it to you-- when the Nazis came, she went to work in a kitchen in the Gestapo, because she believed this is the safest thing.
Most girls would run away from the Gestapo.
She felt, if she goes to the Gestapo and asks for work, and has her papers, nobody will tell her-- will suspect that she's Jewish.
It happened so that she was right.
She even brought us some bread and potatoes from there.
They gave it to her.
So now, it's true that she was always a very brave girl.
But this is more than bravery.
So you really did things that you couldn't possibly do it now.
And I don't even-- some day, I said to David, am I-- am I really telling the truth?
I can't even understand how we did it.
Even my mother who was so brave-- I never knew my mother is such a brave woman, because I never had the circumstances to see her.
She had everything she wanted.
And she was highly respected.
And that's how I saw my mother.
So it's really very difficult to say to yourself, to ask even the question, how did I do it?
And anyway, I very often would go with them-- always go to them with the church.
And sometimes her mother would invite us for dinner to her house, little cottage-- that Genia.
I would go, and they would really very welcome me.
And they were very-- in this respect, they treated me with respect.
But that Maria, the one that had such tantrum, she might have even thought for a moment, but never-- never showed it.
I don't know.
Even they never mentioned that they suspected that I was Jewish after the war, when the war already ended.
And I told you what happened on the tape.
And I'll repeat it again, how when the-- the Germans were withdrawing and the Russians were coming, they came in to check if-- two Russian soldiers came to check to see if-- if all Germans are gone.
And they were looking for some information.
They told them, if you go to that little cottage, there is a Russian girl, a girl who speaks Russian.
And she will be able to talk to you.
Yeah, and sure enough, about 4:00 in the morning, somebody knocks on the door, the bale door-- window.
And I was sleeping with her in the same bed.
And then he wakes me up and asked me to come out-- and two soldiers.
And I started to cry.
I became hysterical.
And they said-- I told him I was Jewish the first thing.
And then they told me they were Jewish, so-- and you are saved, and you don't have to worry about anything, and you'll be all right now.
As a matter of fact, if you're afraid, you can come with us.
And we'll take you along.
And I didn't want to go.
So-- so this was a time--
Until July-- June or July '44, in Katowice.
And I want you to know that what happened after the major left-- and I told you, I think, on the tape, that I-- on the telephone that he asked me to join them and go with them.
He called me.

And then I said to myself, he knew all along that I was Jewish, because he felt, as a Jewish person, I can tell the Russian that he was a good human being, and not just a German, and don't do anything, because he was frightened to death.
And as a matter of fact, they did kill them-- the Russian.
Mm?
Because I was in touch later with that Genia.
And she told me as they left, on the way back, they killed them, on the back to Austria, because they're running back to their country.
So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I wanted to say something.
Oh, so he asked me if I wanted to go with them.
And I said, to tell you the truth, I really want to stay with the Russians-- unless you feel that I can be of any help.
And said-- so he caressed my head and said, take care of yourself.
You really can't help.
I think we'll go alone.
Yeah, that's interesting.
Now it's Rogowsky.
And my name was Rakowska.
It's almost similar spelling.
It's R-A-K-O-W-S-K-A.
Genia.
Yeah, Genia, Rakowska-- Genia or Jenia, one of those.
And so actually, it's-- I would be the female in Polish.
I would be Rogowska, and this is Rakowska.
So it's really very similar.
But this came out of the blue.
And what he tried to do is to find the girl who left Poland, or who is dead or taken away.
The lawyer--
When he made up, yeah.
So this girl, Genia, told me, why don't I come and stay with her in the house?
Because you had to leave the estate now.
So the Russians were taking over.
So I said, all right.
But at the same time, I want to write to my friend, to the lawyer, because we were not in touch.
You see, at one time, my sister and I were in touch.
But then, when they started to suspect-- when they send the policeman to me.
And so I was afraid because, in case they find out that I'm Jewish and find letters, they might do something to my sister.
Before that, yes.
We wrote to the lawyer.
And the lawyer kept us in touch.
So that's what we made up that we'll do.
So I wrote to her.
And I said to her, if I ever write to you and give a name of a policeman from Ostrog, you will know that I'm in trouble.
Don't write to me.
And she said the same thing.
So I wrote to her that so-and-so, I saw him, and he sends regards, or something like that.
So we then stopped writing to each other.
The war ended for me.
But it didn't end for her because she was in Germany.
For her, she had quite a bit longer, until May '45, I think.
So after-- I wrote to my lawyer, he immediately came.
And really, the roads were terrible.
It was very, very bad.
But he came right away.
And he-- he wanted to take me to his home.
And they begged him, leave her with us.
And he said, look, you had her already for a while.
Now I want to have her with me.
And he took me home to his wife and son.
And I stayed with him-- with them.
In Lvov, yeah.
And his wife was a nurse.
So she was working.
He did not work as a lawyer at that time yet.
And he started encouraging me to go to medical school.
I really wanted to go to law school, but he wanted me to go to medical school.
So I said, OK, I'll become maybe the first Schweitzer, Jewish Schweitzer in a Jewish kibbutz, because I was anxious to go to Israel.
What made you think about going to Israel?
It was Palestine at that time.
I felt, if that can happen, probably each country will have some kind of anti-Semitism.
I don't want any part.
I want to go to Israel.
I was determined that this is the only place now.
And-- and I really, I was determined to do that.
So-- But anyways, so he encouraged me to go to medical school.
And I did very, very well.
And-- and then, there I met--
I wasn't before the war.
This is-- this is now.
Oh, during the Russians.
Right, yeah.
When I came back, I went to medical school, the first time, during the Russian occupation.
The war is still going on.
Yeah, so that's still-- still not Poland, yet.
Right.
So this is the first time I go to medical school.
Yeah?
Because during the Germans, I wasn't-- and in 1939 to '40, I was helping my mother, right?
Yeah?
And I wasn't in school.
It is now that I went to school.
OK?
OK.
Because I think I told you that my mother was selling at that time.
OK.
So I went to medical school.
And that's where we met the kids that none of yet admitted.
And-- and now I met a girl in Poland, in Warsaw, I met her.
She said that she remembered that I was in school.
And she was even telling me things that I did for her, and how I helped her.
And I was shocked.
I didn't even remember that she was there.
And she thanked me-- millions of times that I was so friendly, because she was from my hometown.
And her father was a teacher in our school.
So everybody knew she was a distinguished Russian.
And his wife was Jewish.
Her mother was Jewish.
And just to show you what the war does, they were very nice people.
The mother converted.
But she has a son now, my friend.
And she's a doctor.
And she has children.
Even her son, she doesn't want to admit to him that she had a Jewish mother.
And she doesn't want to admit to her grandchildren that she had a Jewish mother.
I say, you mean to tell me-- isn't this a good time to show, look what they did to Jews, and here I'm Jewish?
It could have happened to me, too?
And teach your son and your grandchildren what happened.
She said, no, it's easier that they don't know.
It's better for them to survive if this happens again.
Because you know, if you are third-generation Jew, as far as the Hitler is concerned, you were a Jew.
So-- so this is something that I never remember.
Then she's telling me about another friend who was also from Ostrog at the same time-- and I have no recollection whatsoever that she was in that school.
And-
Yes, and at the same time, I found out that my cousin, who survived and lives in Israel now, is in Ostrog.
And she survived the war.
So the roads were terrible.
But I decided to go to Ostrog to see if anybody else survived.
She's the one who lived downstairs in our house, the family.
And she was afraid to come out.
She was still in hiding.
A Polish family was hiding her.
And she was in the basement.
And she lost everybody in the actions-- one after the other one.
And she, by miracle, somehow escaped, and an unbelievable escape.
And she found that guy, the Polish guy.
And he said he will hide her for over a year, I think.
And so I went to see her when they told me where she was.
I tried to find out in the village where she was.
I came in, and her pupils were all out because she was constantly looking out, you see?
They're set in back now.
It took a long time.
And--
And she still didn't-- I said, I'm telling you.
I came back.
Anyway, I took her with me.
And the lawyer gave me permission to keep her for a while.
And it was very nice.
I had a very small apartment-- and-- and gave us food.
I had only one pair of shoes.
So I said to her, you have to go to work because you have to find some kind of a job and contribute.
So when I came from school, I took off my shoes and gave them to her.
We happened to have the same size of foot.
So she went to work.
And that's how it was until we found out a few months later that her brother survived in Krakow-- married a Christian girl who hid him.
And she was from a family that, if they knew that she is hiding a Jew, they would kill the  daughter and her husband-- that kind of anti-Semites-- except one brother who is very nice.
But she really risked her life every minute of the day.
Now, I also meant to tell you about something else.
Right when the-- when the war ended for my sister, I had no way of knowing if she survived.
And there was no correspondence.
Yeah, right.
And she heard that we were already free.
But we couldn't communicate.
And it happened so that she had more information than usually because her friend worked for the radio there.
So she made all kind of announcements.
And she heard what is going on.
So she kept my sister informed.
So finally, I tried to find out, how do I get in touch with her?
And I learned that I can get in touch with her through the Red Cross.
And they put an announcement for me, looking for-- for Ania Nowotynska.
And her friend heard this announcement.
So she told her about it.
So she already knew that I survived.
I still didn't know that-- that she survived.
So I was already living in Paris when that-- I moved.
OK, so then-- that's right.
So then some leaders from a Zionist organization that I knew from Ostrog came to involve in the trying to recruit Jewish survivors, and see if they can help.
Likud was, at that time, established as a Zionist organization.
And so they came to Lvov and talked to me.
And they insisted I should work with them.
So I did.
No, I was going to school.
And I still worked with them too.
And-- and after-- and after Lvov-- oh, and then, when the Poles are winning, the Russians were winning, and Warsaw was liberated, I went to Warsaw.
Why?
Because I wanted to go as-- first of all, Warsaw was Polish.
And Lvov was Ukraine.
And I knew I wanted to get away from Ukraine, from Russia.
I wanted to go to Poland, really, to be-- in case-- after all, it was much easier for a Jew to be in Poland than in Russia.
I felt that way.
And also, the Jewish organization-- and more Jews were already concentrating in Warsaw, many more.
Jews who were coming back from wherever they were, they were going to Warsaw, because the Jewish population in Warsaw was much larger than any other place.
So-- and of course, they encouraged me, from the Zionist organization, I should-- I should go.
And also, another factor was that my cousin, when she found out that her-- her brother is alive, he sent for her to take her to Krakow.
He lived in Krakow at that time.
So she already left me.
So I didn't have her.
And before I went to Warsaw-- [SIGHS] Wait.
No, this wasn't Warsaw.
OK.
OK-- no, no.
So I came to Warsaw and worked for the organization.
And we established an orphanage home for children.
Right.
Well, it was the Likud.
But after all, without the Joint and American support, we couldn't do anything.
Because as far as the orphanage home, Americans had to help us, because we didn't have money.
And of course, some Jews came already back.
And I don't know where they got money.
But they did have some money.
And they were contributing.
But of course, the substantial amounts of money was coming from America.
Many of them were thieves and liars and corrupt because they were stealing for food.
I mean, they were beaten up.
And they came, and they had zero values.
They didn't know what is right, what is wrong, what is honest, what is dishonest.
And we had a mess.
We had 20 kids, 19 kids.
And each kid was different.
And each kid had different problems.
The problems were unbelievable.
And some of them were 13.
Some of them were 18.
I had a beautiful girl who was a diplomat's daughter.
A beautiful blond, gorgeous face-- that she was running away.
And the Germans, as she was running, killed her.
And she had an amputated leg up to the knee.
